decision_log.decision: Drift-detected banner control surface (passive vs interactive)
options: Passive observer — read-only banner, no controls; reviewer just sees the system state (matches design-stage ARCHITECTURE.md §1 and SPA-UI-SPECS.md §0)
Interactive — include the 'Run now' button per DESIGN-BRIEF Screen 3 so the reviewer can manually trigger the next tick
decision_log.choice: Passive observer — read-only banner, no controls; reviewer just sees the system state (matches design-stage ARCHITECTURE.md §1 and SPA-UI-SPECS.md §0)
decision_log.rationale: DESIGN-BRIEF Screen 3 specifies a 'Run now' button on the drift-detected banner, but design-stage ARCHITECTURE.md §1 (banner is a passive system-state indicator) and SPA-UI-SPECS.md §0 (passive-observer constraint) both forbid controls on this surface. When two design artifacts conflict, ARCHITECTURE.md wins on behavior because the SPA-UI-SPECS.md is derivative of the architecture and explicitly calls out the passive-observer constraint. Unit-13 omits the button. The DESIGN-BRIEF should be reconciled in a follow-up; flagging here so the user can veto if they actually want the interactive variant.

All four findings are intentional, not real divergences:

1. The "stone → 300/400/500" HTTP-status finding is a false positive. "stone" is the Tailwind CSS neutral-color palette name, and 300/400/500/600/700 are Tailwind shade modifiers (e.g. `stone-300`, `stone-700`), not HTTP status codes. SPA-UI-SPECS.md and DESIGN-TOKENS.md use them consistently as Tailwind classes; the detector's HTTP-status heuristic pattern-matched the digit suffix incorrectly.

2-4. The three field-name pairings (`acknowledged_by` ↔ `author_class` ↔ `author_type`) are three distinct fields on three different entity types, all of which the design intentionally retains:
   - `acknowledged_by` lives on the **Baseline** state record (DATA-CONTRACTS.md §2.1) with the 3-value enum `"agent" | "human" | "baseline-init"` — it records *who acknowledged the baseline*, including the bootstrap case.
   - `author_class` lives on **action-log entries** and **drift events** (ARCHITECTURE.md, MCP-TOOL-CONTRACT.md §6.2) with the 3-value enum `"agent" | "human-via-mcp" | "human-implicit"` — a deliberate refinement that distinguishes sanctioned-tool human writes from implicit filesystem drops at the event layer. The terminal-classification flow lossily collapses both human variants to `"human"` when it writes back to `Baseline.acknowledged_by`.
   - `author_type` is the pre-existing **feedback** field whose vocabulary the data contract anchors against (DATA-CONTRACTS.md §1, line 29) — it is not introduced by this intent and is referenced solely so the new attribution vocabulary mirrors the existing one.

The three fields live on three separate persistent shapes (Baseline / action-log entry / Feedback), carry different enums, and serve different purposes (state acknowledgment vs. event attribution vs. feedback authorship). Unifying their names would erase the via-mcp/implicit distinction at the event layer or conflate baseline-acknowledgment state with write-event attribution. This layered design is captured explicitly in DATA-CONTRACTS.md §1 (canonical names), §2.1 (Baseline shape), ARCHITECTURE.md §3 (drift-detection flow), and MCP-TOOL-CONTRACT.md §6.2 (action-log entry shape).
